text: I'm sharing a ETEP RIV-STOPA on a patient who had a upper midline laparotomy for a partial gastrectomy for a GI stromal tumor.
That was a laparoscopic converted to an open procedure. I think that there was some bleeding during that operation from what I was told.
He's a muscular guy and he developed kind of a Swiss cheese defect.
This scan is actually from close to a year prior and you can see the Swiss cheese defects there throughout the upper midline and I selected the patient for an ETEP Reef Stopa for my repair.
I did not rescan him prior to surgery.
So I'm doing a right-sided doc.

My plan initially was to do a lower midline crossover to try to avoid directly accessing the upper hernia defects. And I was even contemplating, after I crossed over the lower midline, doing a lower doc aiming to upper.
What you'll see is during the case, I just kept going to different locations and working where it was easy. I ended up doing everything from the three right-sided ports.
The surgeon develops the retroactive space using a robotic eight millimeter trocar, accessing the area directly through the port.
I left this video largely unedited. It is a little bit long.
It was mostly so that I could continue to show my steps in case anyone has suggestions on efficiency or other tricks.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection using a Storz 5.0 camera, maneuvering the camera to separate tissue layers.
The surgeon creates space to place a second trocar lateral to the semilunar line.
I do mark out the semilunar lines bilaterally with ultrasound prior to the case.
The surgeon adjusts the operating table to extend the patient's position, aiding in the separation of the costal margin from the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS).
The surgeon positions the second trocar lateral to the semilunar line, using a spinal needle to assist in placement.
The surgeon positions the second trocar, aiming laterally to the semilunar line and inserting it just medial to this anatomical landmark.
The surgeon switches to using a robotic camera and performs additional dissection to create space for the placement of the third trocar.
The arcuate line is visible in the surgical field, marking the transition between the posterior rectus sheath and the transversalis fascia.
The surgeon identifies the epigastric vessels, which are visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon places the last trocar, a bariatric length trocar, near the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) for better clearance.
The surgeon positions the trocar, visible near the semi lunar line, and proceeds to dock the robotic system for the hernia procedure.
The surgeon clears the midline from top to bottom, using robotic assistance to dissect and expose the area along the midline.
And again, my plan was to do a lower midline crossover.
The surgeon is positioned below the arcuate line, with the midline visible. The rectus muscle is clearly identifiable adjacent to the midline.
The surgeon navigates up against the rectus muscles, crossing the midline in the lower abdomen. The patient's rectus muscles appear scalloped and form a teepee-like shape due to his muscular build.

The surgeon exposes the contralateral rectus muscle, which appears almost vertical, and proceeds to dissect through the posterior sheath above the arcuate line. The dissection continues down through the arcuate line, clearing the ipsilateral posterior sheath using a bottoms-up approach.
The surgeon continues the dissection below the umbilicus, crossing the midline while maintaining proximity to the rectus muscles.
The surgical view is focused on the lower left quadrant below the arcuate line, within the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon attempts to dissect from an inferior to superior direction, navigating through the preperitoneal space below the arcuate line, with visible rectus muscles forming a scalloped appearance.
The surgeon opens the entire ipsilateral posterior rectus sheath, attempting to visualize and assess the crossing over of anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon reaches the subxiphoid area above the hernia defect, facilitating easier crossing over of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully works through the hernia defect area, avoiding the creation of holes to prevent gaps in the posterior elements, while crossing over the midline.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers along the posterior aspect of the fascial defects, maintaining close proximity to the tissue to avoid creating additional openings.
The surgeon navigates to the back of the rectus muscle and enters the left-sided retro-rectus space, preparing to work within this anatomical area.
The surgeon continues to navigate and dissect across multiple small defects in the diastatic rectus linea alba, resembling a Swiss cheese pattern.
A small hole is visible in the lateral peritoneum, resulting in the loss of gas into the peritoneal cavity and a reduction in the working space.
The surgeon applies increased pressure with the left hand to maintain space within the surgical field, aiming to prevent further loss of working area during the hernia repair.
Other things you can do is you can Make the peritoneal hole a little bit wider. You could drop a varus needle or a five millimeter trocar into the peritoneal cavity and vent.
The surgeon pushes down to create a larger working space within the left-sided retro-rectus area, allowing for improved visibility and maneuverability.
The surgeon works from the inferior part of the hernia defects, reducing the Swiss cheese defects in the diastatic rectus linea alba, before moving back up to the top.

The surgeon identifies one of the larger hernia defects and uses cold scissors for dissection, carefully preserving the peritoneum and hernia sac to prevent thermal spread to the contents on the peritoneal side.

The surgeon inspects the inside of the peritoneal hole to ensure there are no concerning structures. The dissection progresses smoothly, with the hernia defect nearly completely reduced. The hernia sac is carefully taken along to avoid creating large holes in the posterior elements.
The surgeon examines the surgical field and proceeds to take down the remaining midline hernia defects, ensuring complete reduction of the hernia sac.
The surgeon inspects the hernia site, observing omentum without any visible bowel involvement, ensuring the area is safe for continued dissection.

The surgeon demonstrates the 'volcano sign' while crossing the midline and clearing all hernia defects. The left posterior rectus sheath is being opened, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon develops the left-sided posterior rectus space and retrovector space, following the completion of previous dissection steps.
The surgeon carefully clears the entire left-sided posterior rectus space, paying close attention to avoid injuring the neurovascular bundles located laterally.
The surgeon identifies neurovascular bundles within the surgical field, ensuring careful navigation around these structures.
The surgeon identifies the epigastric vessels within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs additional dissection in the space of Retzius, avoiding further inferior dissection below the pubic tubercle due to the absence of a lower midline hernia.
The surgeon performs additional upper dissection to create space for a few more centimeters of mesh placement superiorly.
The surgeon examines the holes in the posterior component of the layers and begins closing them using a two-ovelock suture.
The surgeon preserves most of the posterior peritoneum and hernia sac, ensuring that the closure does not create tension in the posterior area.

Obviously it's always a concern that you can have tension on your closure and maybe the stitches pop or one of these little holes opens up and a loop of bowel can sneak in there. But I was very happy with how this came together. I wasn't concerned about any tension whatsoever
The surgeon begins anterior closure using a zero V-Lock suture, starting from the top of the surgical field.

The surgeon approaches the widest hernia defect, reducing the pressure to about eight to facilitate closure. The anterior sheath is brought together over the defect, which appears wider than anticipated, without visible tension.
The surgeon incorporates part of the hernia defect pseudosac into the closure, aiming to reduce the dead space within the surgical field.

The surgeon encounters a small bleeding vessel and decides to use image inversion for the first time, flipping the camera view and reassigning instruments to switch hands, adjusting the orientation of the surgical field.

I just wanted to see truly how much of a difference it made. And I will tell you that I actually was surprised how much it just seemed easier to suture this way, even though technically the view is the same, just the difference between feeling like you're suturing on the ceiling backhanded to suturing forehanded just intuitively was actually more natural. And so I'm looking forward to doing this more frequently. Is it the biggest game changer in the world? Probably not, but it really did help in this case. I enjoyed it.

The surgeon observes the scalloped rectus muscles, which curve inward, partially obstructing the view of the fascial opening. The posterior sheath rim is left intact on the ipsilateral side to maintain visibility, preventing retraction behind the muscle near the linea alba.
The surgeon uses their left hand to hold down the muscle for better visualization while completing a stitch at the lower portion of the hernia near the pubic symphysis.
The scalloped rectus muscles are visible, leading down to the fascial defect located in the midline valley.
The surgeon ties two stitches together where they meet and continues suturing from the inferior position towards the superior direction.
The surgeon passes a barbed suture through the hernia defect closure, a technique used in these cases to potentially enhance closure, despite uncertainties about its impact on strength or ischemia.
The surgeon measures the hernia repair space, noting dimensions of approximately 32 to 33 centimeters in length and about 20 centimeters in width.
The surgeon cuts a piece of heavyweight polypropylene mesh, initially measuring thirty by thirty centimeters, down to thirty by twenty centimeters.
The surgeon inserts a 12mm laparoscopic trocar into the right upper quadrant port site to accommodate the insertion of the polypropylene mesh, having previously used three 8mm trocars.

The camera is positioned in the lower port, aiming towards the right upper quadrant. The surgeon flattens a portion of the heavyweight polypropylene mesh in the right upper quadrant, ensuring it is properly positioned within the subxiphoid space.

And with that, we desufflate. I did not leave a drain. Patient was discharged as an outpatient. I do not believe that I sent them home on any narcotics. And the patient did very well and has been very happy with the result. I hope you enjoyed.
